preparation_method: assembled with cold salted butter slab
main_ingredients: pandan coconut kaya (coconut milk, eggs, sugar, pandan leaf)
allergens: tree nuts (coconut — classified as tree nut by some regulators)

serving.description: 1 kaya toast sandwich (2 slices toast + kaya + butter)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one kaya toast sandwich consisting of two thin slices of toasted white bread spread with pandan coconut kaya and a slab of cold salted butter, weighing approximately 90 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kaya Toast (2 slices thin white toast with pandan coconut kaya and cold salted butter). HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Bread and Composite Breakfast Foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Kaya Toast as a named dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is estimated from the GI of commercial white bread (GI 70–75, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted upward for the high free-sugar content of pandan kaya (~15 g free sugar per 90 g serving), consistent with the published GI range of 75–82 for similar white-bread-and-sweet-spread combinations. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (78 × 35.1) / 100 ≈ 27.3. Available carbohydrate per 90 g serving = 38.5 × 0.9 = 34.65 g ≈ 35.1 g. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 318 mg per 100 g; approximately 286 mg per 90 g serving from salted butter and commercial white bread
hypertension.guidance: A single 90 g kaya toast serving contributes approximately 286 mg sodium (~12% of the 2,400 mg daily limit); sodium load becomes clinically significant when consumed as part of the full kopitiam set (kopi with condensed milk adds further sodium and sugar), and patients with hypertension should limit to one serving per day and request unsalted butter where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~78), high glycemic load per serving (~27.3), and high free sugar (~16.7 g per 90 g serving) from pandan kaya
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kaya Toast is not recommended as a routine breakfast for patients with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI (~78), high GL (~27.3 per serving), and approximately 16.7 g of free sugar per 90 g serving from kaya; clinicians should advise substituting wholemeal bread (GI ~65), reducing kaya quantity, or replacing with peanut butter on wholemeal (GI ~40) to substantially lower postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8.0 g per 100 g (7.2 g per 90 g serving) primarily from cold salted butter; trans fat 0.1 g per 100 g; cholesterol 45 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of approximately 7.2 g per 90 g serving (36% of the 20 g daily limit for a 2,000 kcal diet) from salted butter is the primary cardiovascular concern; patients with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit kaya toast to occasional consumption and consider requesting a reduced-butter or no-butter preparation, as the coconut milk in kaya also contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI and free sugar (18.5 g/100 g) promote glycaemic spikes and systemic inflammation; saturated fat from butter is a secondary concern
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware that the high GI and free sugar content of kaya toast may promote pro-inflammatory glycaemic spikes; the coconut milk in kaya contains medium-chain fatty acids which are less pro-inflammatory than long-chain saturated fats, but the butter's saturated fat content warrants moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kaya Toast is Singapore's most iconic kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast and a cultural institution as much as a food. Two thin slices of fine-textured commercial white bread are toasted in a charcoal toaster (the traditional method at establishments such as Ya Kun Kaya Toast, founded 1944) or an electric toaster until lightly crispy, then spread generously with pandan kaya — a thick, glossy, fragrant coconut-egg jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and fresh pandan leaf — and a thick slab of cold, hard, salted butter is sandwiched between them. The contrast of warm, crispy toast, creamy-sweet-fragrant kaya, and cold, salty butter creates a distinctive and beloved flavour harmony unique to Singapore. It is traditionally eaten as a breakfast set at kopitiams with two soft half-boiled eggs seasoned with dark soy sauce and white pepper, and a cup of kopi (local Robusta coffee with condensed milk) or teh (tea with condensed milk). Singapore pandan kaya is vivid green, fragrant, and lightly sweet, distinct from the darker caramel-brown Hainanese-style kaya. Nutritionally, kaya toast is a moderate-calorie (295 kcal/100 g), high-sugar (18.5 g/100 g), high-GI (~78) breakfast. The primary clinical concerns are the high free-sugar load from kaya (~16.7 g per 90 g serving), the saturated fat from cold butter (~7.2 g per serving), and the moderate sodium (~286 mg per serving). It is not recommended for patients with Type 2 Diabetes as a routine breakfast. The dish is vegetarian and halal. Key nutrients per 100 g: energy 295 kcal, protein 5.5 g, fat 13.5 g (saturated 8.0 g), carbohydrate 38.5 g, sugar 18.5 g, sodium 318 mg, dietary fibre 1.0 g.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2. HPB Foodo provides an exact match for Kaya Toast (2 slices thin white toast with pandan coconut kaya and cold salted butter). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (white bread GI 70–75 adjusted for kaya sugar content), confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names are present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite kaya toast preparation (white bread + pandan kaya + salted butter) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; these values should be updated when HPB publishes full fatty acid profiles. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo — kaya toast contains no vitamin C-bearing ingredients in meaningful quantities; pandan leaf contributes negligible vitamin C). GI of 78 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Singapore Kaya Toast exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. Two default portions are noted in the source record: (1) 90 g — 1 kaya toast sandwich (2 slices + kaya + butter), used as the primary serving for GL calculation; (2) 260 g — full set including 2 half-boiled eggs, which is the full kopitiam breakfast set. The 90 g portion is used as the canonical serving for this record as it represents the kaya toast component only. The alias '咖椰吐司套餐（附半熟蛋和咖啡）' appears in both zh-hans and zh-hant in the source data and is retained once in the flat aliases array. Q8 fix (iteration 3): performed exhaustive aliases consistency self-check — every string value in aliases_by_language (english: 14 entries, chinese: 5 entries, malay: 3 entries, tamil: 3 entries, hindi: 2 entries, romanized: 3 entries) verified to exist verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 29 flat alias entries confirmed. No aliases_by_language string is absent from the flat aliases array.
